Deployment Model / SKU: Single VM or multi-VM across zones
HA Capability Summary: Azure VMs can use availability zones, but VM-level zone resiliency is customer designed rather than fully managed. Zone failure handling requires traffic rerouting and operational response by the customer.
DR Capability Summary: VMs are single-region resources. For regional DR, deploy to another region and use Azure Site Recovery or a custom multi-region pattern with replication and failover.
DR Pattern Type: Azure Site Recovery to secondary region
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Supports Cross Region Replication: Customer-implemented or dependent on disk choice
Replication Type: Application and data dependent; Site Recovery replicates VM workload state
Failover Type: Manual for zone events; manual or orchestrated for DR
Expected RTO Range: Zone failure downtime depends on workload failover design; region outage requires failover to secondary region
Expected RPO Range: Zone-level RPO depends on app and storage design; regional DR depends on replication settings
Recommended For: Stateful workloads that need infrastructure control and can absorb operational complexity
Not Recommended For: Teams expecting PaaS-style automatic zone failover without runbooks
Key Assumptions: Multiple VMs or alternate capacity exist in healthy zones or regions
Dependencies: Load balancer, app/data replication, DNS, monitoring, runbooks
Limitations: Single zonal VM remains down until zone recovery; Site Recovery zone-to-zone has limitations; customer owns rerouting
Test / Drill Guidance: Test with Chaos Studio for zone loss and exercise Site Recovery failover and failback regularly
Notes / Interpretation: HA for VMs is architecture-dependent; zone support is not the same as true DR.

Deployment Model / SKU: General Purpose, Premium, Business Critical, Hyperscale
HA Capability Summary: Azure SQL Database includes built-in platform HA. Zone redundancy raises resiliency to zonal failures within a region and Azure handles zone failover automatically.
DR Capability Summary: For regional DR, use active geo-replication, failover groups, or geo-restore. Failover groups are generally the cleaner multi-database DR option because listener endpoints remain stable.
HA Pattern Type: Built-in HA with optional zone redundancy
DR Pattern Type: Failover groups or active geo-replication; geo-restore for slower recovery
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: Zone redundancy is intra-region; DR options use continuous sync or geo-replicated backups
Failover Type: Automatic for zonal HA; customer-managed failover policy for DR patterns
Expected RTO Range: HA with zone redundancy is typically under 30 seconds per Microsoft for zone failover; DR fit depends on chosen feature
Expected RPO Range: HA RPO is effectively zero for zone failover; DR RPO for failover groups or active geo-replication depends on async replication lag
Recommended For: Mission-critical SQL workloads needing managed HA and strong DR options
Not Recommended For: Workloads that need guaranteed synchronous cross-region zero-loss semantics
Key Assumptions: Service tier and region support are validated before recommendation
Dependencies: Client retry logic, failover policy, DNS/listener usage, app connection resiliency
Limitations: Zone redundancy is HA not regional DR; DR precision is feature and workload dependent
Test / Drill Guidance: Run failover drills, validate application retry behavior, and test geo-restore separately from failover groups
Notes / Interpretation: Do not label zone redundancy as DR; true regional DR starts with cross-region patterns.

HA Capability Summary: App Service supports zone redundancy on supported Premium plans. The platform handles zone failure detection and traffic redistribution inside the region.
DR Capability Summary: App Service is a single-region service. Regional DR requires duplicating the app in another region and combining it with traffic management and replicated dependencies.
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: App file system state is synchronously replicated across zones when zone redundant; cross-region data handling depends on dependent services
Failover Type: Automatic for zone events; customer-managed for regional failover
Expected RTO Range: Zone-redundant plans continue serving traffic during zone issues, but regional outage makes the app unavailable until traffic is shifted to another region
Expected RPO Range: HA RPO is tied to platform-managed zone redundancy for app state; DR RPO depends on cross-region data replication strategy
Recommended For: HTTP apps that can keep state outside the web tier and duplicate deployment across regions
Not Recommended For: Apps that keep critical state only on local App Service storage
Key Assumptions: Premium tier, supported scale unit, and minimum two instances are available
Dependencies: Traffic manager or Front Door, replicated data tier, configuration sync, certificates and secrets in both regions
Limitations: Not all scale units support zone redundancy; overprovisioning is recommended if capacity must survive zone loss
Test / Drill Guidance: Test regional failover through the chosen traffic routing layer and verify deployment parity between regions
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Multi-region App Service app approaches for disaster recovery
Notes / Interpretation: App Service zone redundancy is HA; DR is always a broader multi-region application pattern.

Deployment Model / SKU: LRS, ZRS, GRS, GZRS, RA-GRS, RA-GZRS
Workload Type: Object, queue, table, file, lake storage
HA Capability Summary: Storage redundancy settings determine local and zonal resiliency. Microsoft recommends ZRS in the primary region for high availability and for Data Lake Storage workloads.
DR Capability Summary: Regional DR comes from GRS or GZRS with failover, and optional read access comes from RA-GRS or RA-GZRS.
HA Pattern Type: LRS or ZRS depending HA need
DR Pattern Type: GRS or GZRS with failover; RA variants for readable secondary
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region when geo options are used
Supports Cross Region Replication: Yes with GRS GZRS RA variants
Replication Type: Synchronous in-region for ZRS; asynchronous to secondary for GRS or GZRS
Failover Type: Automatic within region; regional failover is customer-managed or Microsoft-managed depending scenario
Expected RTO Range: Local or zonal HA is immediate based on redundancy choice; regional failover timing depends on failover process
Expected RPO Range: ZRS has no regional copy; GRS and GZRS are asynchronous cross-region designs so some lag can exist
Recommended For: Storage-centric workloads where redundancy choice can be aligned to business RTO and RPO
Not Recommended For: Teams assuming LRS or ZRS alone provide regional DR
Key Assumptions: Applications tolerate the chosen replication lag and failover model
Dependencies: Failover runbook, application read/write endpoint handling, testing, DNS and secondary-read design
Limitations: Azure Files does not support RA-GRS or RA-GZRS; secondary region access differs by redundancy option
Test / Drill Guidance: Exercise planned failover where supported and validate client behavior against secondary endpoints
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Diagram showing how data is replicated with GRS or RA-GRS
Notes / Interpretation: Same-region redundancy is HA, while geo-redundancy is the storage DR boundary.

HA Capability Summary: Service Bus provides built-in zone resilience behavior and automatically reroutes to healthy replicas during zone issues.
DR Capability Summary: For region outages, Premium tier supports Geo-Replication for metadata plus data and metadata Geo-Disaster Recovery for metadata only. Microsoft recommends Geo-Replication for most DR scenarios.
DR Pattern Type: Geo-Replication preferred; metadata Geo-Disaster Recovery only when metadata-only continuity is acceptable
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: Geo-Replication can be synchronous or asynchronous; Geo-DR is metadata-only
Failover Type: Managed within zone; regional promotion/failover is manual
Expected RTO Range: Zone issues are service-managed; DR timing depends on manual promotion and client switchover
Expected RPO Range: Geo-Replication can avoid data loss in planned promotion; metadata Geo-DR does not replicate messages
Recommended For: Critical messaging systems needing managed broker semantics and clear regional recovery options
Not Recommended For: Workloads that assume metadata Geo-DR preserves queued messages
Key Assumptions: Premium tier is acceptable and application understands alias and promotion model
Dependencies: RBAC, networking, aliases, runbooks, and downstream message durability strategy
Limitations: Standard and Basic lack native multi-region features; Service Bus itself has no backup and restore for message data
Test / Drill Guidance: Run planned promotion tests and validate message durability expectations before calling a design enterprise ready
Notes / Interpretation: Do not present metadata Geo-DR as data-protecting DR.

Deployment Model / SKU: Single-region, single-write multi-region, or multi-write multi-region account
HA Capability Summary: A single-region account with availability zones can survive a single-zone outage in-region.
DR Capability Summary: Regional recovery behavior depends on account topology. Single-region accounts may need restore to another region if outage exceeds RTO. Multi-region accounts can reroute reads and, with multi-write or PPAF patterns, preserve higher availability.
HA Pattern Type: Availability Zones for single-region HA; multi-region replicas for broader HA
DR Pattern Type: Multi-region account design; region offline operation or restore depending configuration
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region and cross region depending account design; DR cross region
Replication Type: Replication depends on account configuration and consistency model
Failover Type: Automatic routing with SDK in multi-region cases; some operations require region offline action
Expected RTO Range: Multi-write accounts can continue through a regional outage with minimal disruption; single-region accounts cannot
Expected RPO Range: RPO and write availability depend heavily on account topology, consistency, and backup mode
Recommended For: Globally distributed apps that can use SDK-aware regional routing and well-planned account topology
Not Recommended For: Single-region critical workloads with aggressive RTO and RPO targets
Key Assumptions: Clients are configured with preferred regions or application region settings
Dependencies: Traffic routing, private DNS behavior, consistency tuning, backup mode, operational runbooks
Limitations: Service-managed failover timing can take one hour or more; restore and online-region actions can take days
Test / Drill Guidance: Test region outage procedures, SDK preferred-region behavior, and restore paths; avoid control-plane changes during outages
Notes / Interpretation: Cosmos DB requires topology-specific recommendations; one account shape cannot represent the whole service.

Workload Type: Global HTTP ingress and CDN edge
HA Capability Summary: Azure Front Door is a globally distributed service designed to route traffic across multiple origins and regions. It supports active-active and active-passive origin strategies with health probes and edge routing.
DR Capability Summary: Front Door itself is highly resilient, but Microsoft explicitly warns it can still be a potential single point of failure for mission-critical workloads. For higher DR assurance, add a secondary ingress path such as Traffic Manager directing to an alternate router.
HA Pattern Type: Global health-probed ingress across multiple origins
DR Pattern Type: Manual failover to alternate ingress path for mission-critical architectures
Same Region or Cross Region: Cross-region by design for app origins; Front Door service is global
Supports Cross Region Replication: Not a data replication service; origin redundancy is customer-managed
Replication Type: Health probes and routing decisions across origins
Failover Type: Automatic across healthy origins; manual for alternate-router DR pattern
Expected RTO Range: For origin failures, failover is near-real-time based on health probes; for Front Door service-level disruption, DR depends on manual alternate path procedures
Expected RPO Range: No data RPO concept for Front Door itself; effective DR depends on origin and data tiers behind it
Recommended For: Internet-facing apps deployed in multiple regions that need global routing and edge security
Not Recommended For: Single-region backends with no redundant origin strategy or teams expecting Front Door alone to satisfy end-to-end DR
Key Assumptions: Origins exist in multiple regions and expose solid health endpoints
Dependencies: Origin health endpoints, WAF, TLS and certificate strategy, origin security model, alternate router if required
Limitations: Front Door is not the whole DR strategy; it depends on origin resilience and may need redundant routing only for mission-critical workloads
Test / Drill Guidance: Test origin failover, health probe behavior, and any Traffic Manager manual failover runbook for alternate ingress
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Global routing redundancy for mission-critical web applications
Notes / Interpretation: Front Door improves global availability but should not be treated as the only DR layer for mission-critical ingress.

HA Capability Summary: Application Gateway v2 is a managed regional service with at least two instances for HA. Zone redundancy can protect against zone failure within supported regions.
DR Capability Summary: Regional DR is not built in. Protect against a region outage by duplicating the gateway and backend stack in another region and placing a global routing layer such as Front Door or Traffic Manager in front.
HA Pattern Type: Built-in multi-instance HA with optional zone redundancy
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Supports Availability Zones: Yes on v2 in supported regions
Replication Type: No cross-region replication in the service itself
Expected RTO Range: In-region HA is platform-managed; regional recovery depends on upstream routing and duplicated regional deployment
Expected RPO Range: Application Gateway is stateless so RPO is driven by backend configuration rather than the gateway
Recommended For: Regional web workloads needing Layer 7 routing and WAF close to the app
Not Recommended For: Teams that need native cross-region DR in a single resource
Key Assumptions: v2 SKU is used and backend pools are themselves resilient
Dependencies: Backend health, autoscaling sizing, WAF policy parity, global routing layer for regional DR
Limitations: Gateway resiliency does not cover backend availability; regional DR requires another gateway and another backend footprint
Test / Drill Guidance: Test backend health probe paths, zone outage tolerance, and global failover to a second regional gateway
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Reliability in Azure Application Gateway v2
Notes / Interpretation: Application Gateway is HA inside a region; DR starts only when you add another region and a global traffic director.

Deployment Model / SKU: Premium classic, Premium v2, Standard v2 and others by tier
HA Capability Summary: API Management uses scale units with built-in redundancy. Premium classic supports availability zones and multi-region deployments, while several other tiers are single-datacenter or single-region only.
DR Capability Summary: Regional DR is strongest in Premium classic through multi-region deployment with regional gateways. Other tiers have materially less DR capability and may need backup and restore or customer-managed duplicate deployments.
HA Pattern Type: Multi-unit HA; zone redundancy where supported
DR Pattern Type: Multi-region deployment in Premium classic; otherwise limited customer-managed DR
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region only in supported tiers
Replication Type: Configuration and gateway deployment replicated by service according to tier and region design
Failover Type: Automatic within configured zones and units; regional behavior depends on multi-region setup
Expected RTO Range: In-region HA improves with two or more units and zone support; regional outage tolerance depends on tier and whether extra regions are deployed
Expected RPO Range: RPO precision is not uniformly documented across tiers; fit is based on deployment model rather than guaranteed zero-loss semantics
Recommended For: Enterprise API platforms that can justify Premium classic for multi-region availability
Not Recommended For: Consumption, Developer, or single-region tiers when the business requires strong native DR
Key Assumptions: Tier and region support are validated before recommendation
Dependencies: Scale units, VNet settings, certificate and domain replication, backend resilience, DNS or client routing
Limitations: Premium classic is the only tier explicitly supporting multi-region deployments; not all tiers support zones
Test / Drill Guidance: Exercise region failure scenarios, long-running infrastructure changes, and certificate or domain rollout processes
Notes / Interpretation: APIM recommendations must be tier-specific; avoid implying that all tiers have Premium-class DR features.

Deployment Model / SKU: VpnGw1AZ or higher for zone-aware designs
HA Capability Summary: Azure VPN Gateway uses two gateway VMs and provides active-standby by default, with zone redundancy available on supported SKUs and active-active mode for higher availability.
DR Capability Summary: Regional DR is customer-managed and generally requires another gateway in another region plus resilient on-premises devices and routing. The gateway service alone does not provide full end-to-end DR.
DR Pattern Type: Duplicate regional connectivity design with paired gateways and routing
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: VPN tunnels and routing state across gateway instances; no native cross-region replication abstraction
Failover Type: Automatic failover between gateway instances; customer-managed for regional DR
Expected RTO Range: Active-standby failover causes a brief interruption; active-active and multiple tunnels improve availability
Expected RPO Range: RPO is connectivity-state oriented rather than data-service oriented; application data RPO is external to the gateway
Recommended For: Hybrid networks that need resilient site-to-site or VNet-to-VNet connectivity
Not Recommended For: Architectures that ignore on-premises device resilience or rely on a single tunnel or device
Key Assumptions: Remote devices support active-active or BGP where required
Dependencies: On-premises VPN devices, local network gateways, BGP, routing design, monitoring
Limitations: Overall reliability depends on both Azure gateway and on-premises side; point-to-site reconnect behavior differs from site-to-site
Test / Drill Guidance: Test gateway failover, tunnel loss, and regional rerouting from both Azure and on-premises perspectives
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Design highly available gateway connectivity for cross-premises and VNet-to-VNet connections
Notes / Interpretation: For VPN, HA is topology-specific and regional DR is always a broader network design problem, not a single checkbox.

HA Capability Summary: Virtual WAN provides resiliency inside a hub through active-active gateways and, in the Standard SKU, zone-redundant gateway deployment where supported. It also supports layered redundancy across zones, connections, and routes.
DR Capability Summary: Regional DR requires deploying multiple hubs across regions and planning routing for automatic or manual failover. A single hub is not regional DR.
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: Gateway and route redundancy within hub; cross-region resiliency through multiple hubs
Failover Type: Automatic within a hub; customer-designed region failover
Expected RTO Range: Hub-level failures are masked by active-active and zonal design; region outage recovery depends on extra hubs and routing strategy
Expected RPO Range: RPO is connectivity-state oriented, not a replicated data-store metric; app/data tiers define effective RPO
Recommended For: Enterprise transit networks that need scalable branch, user, and VNet connectivity with resilience layers
Not Recommended For: Single-hub architectures presented as regional DR
Key Assumptions: Virtual WAN Standard is used and routing/failover behavior is designed intentionally
Dependencies: Hub placement, route intent, BGP, gateway sizing, monitoring, branch device resilience
Limitations: Gateways do not auto-scale instantly; routing asymmetry and capacity planning matter during failover
Test / Drill Guidance: Run multihub failover drills, test branch and user path selection, and validate routing under degraded conditions
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Virtual WAN network topology in an Azure landing zone
Notes / Interpretation: Virtual WAN HA is mostly built into hub services, but regional DR is a network topology decision, not a single feature toggle.

HA Capability Summary: Fabric makes commercially reasonable efforts to support availability zones for supported experiences, but support is partial and experience-specific. OneLake-backed experiences inherit stronger resiliency than external data sources.
DR Capability Summary: Fabric cross-region recovery depends on the Fabric DR feature set and customer preparation. OneLake cross-region replication can be enabled, but many experiences fall back to read-only access or require customer actions after failover.
DR Pattern Type: Cross-region recovery with OneLake replication and experience-specific customer actions
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region and DR cross region depending enabled Fabric capabilities
Supports Cross Region Replication: Yes for OneLake-backed scenarios when enabled
Replication Type: Platform replication for OneLake; external stores depend on their own DR
Failover Type: Service-driven failover with customer follow-up actions
Expected RTO Range: HA behavior is strong for supported experiences, but write capability and full functionality can be reduced during failover scenarios
Expected RPO Range: Regional DR fit depends on whether the workload uses OneLake and whether the relevant experience supports DR adequately
Recommended For: Fabric workloads that can tolerate experience-specific limitations during regional failover and can standardize on OneLake
Not Recommended For: Workloads assuming every Fabric experience has identical HA and DR guarantees
Key Assumptions: Capacity settings and DR toggles are enabled where required; critical data is in supported stores
Dependencies: Capacity admin process, gateway behavior, external data stores, workspace and item recovery procedures
Limitations: Availability zone support is partial; many write paths and some experience types have failover limitations
Test / Drill Guidance: Test DR toggles, read-only behavior, gateway dependencies, and experience-specific recovery steps before relying on Fabric for strict DR targets
Notes / Interpretation: Fabric DR must be described per experience, not as one blanket platform guarantee.

HA Capability Summary: Dynamics 365 production environments include built-in in-region resilience across at least two availability zones with near-zero RPO and less than five-minute RTO in most zone-failure cases per Microsoft.
DR Capability Summary: Cross-region DR is app-dependent. Some environments support self-service DR and drills, but support is not universal and several capabilities or products have limitations or are not supported.
HA Pattern Type: Built-in zonal resiliency for production environments
DR Pattern Type: Self-service disaster recovery where supported; otherwise app-specific limitations
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region only where the app and environment support it
Replication Type: In-region synchronous platform protection; cross-region async replication when self-service DR is enabled
Failover Type: Automatic in-region; customer-initiated for self-service DR
Expected RTO Range: Zone failures are designed to recover almost instantly in-region for supported production environments
Expected RPO Range: Cross-region RPO depends on replicated data age shown during failover preparation and whether self-service DR is enabled
Recommended For: Critical production Dynamics environments that use supported apps and managed environment prerequisites
Not Recommended For: Organizations assuming all Dynamics 365 products have uniform self-service DR support
Key Assumptions: Production managed environment is used and app-specific support has been confirmed
Dependencies: Power Platform admin center, integration dependencies, external systems, environment configuration
Limitations: Backups are not cross-region protection unless self-service DR is enabled; some apps remain unsupported or preview-only
Test / Drill Guidance: Run DR drills in supported environments and document external integration recovery steps before an actual outage
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Business continuity and disaster recovery for Dynamics 365 SaaS apps
Notes / Interpretation: D365 has strong built-in in-region resiliency, but cross-region DR is not universally available across all apps.

HA Capability Summary: Official Azure AI reliability guidance is incomplete at the umbrella level and varies by service. Representative guidance from Azure AI Search shows HA comes from multiple replicas and zone-aware placement where supported, while many AI services remain region-specific resources.
DR Capability Summary: Regional DR for Azure AI workloads is generally customer-designed through multi-region deployment, regional feature parity checks, and app-level failover. Microsoft architecture guidance for AI workloads explicitly notes that single-region baseline designs are not DR-ready.
HA Pattern Type: Service-specific built-in HA where supported; often scale-out within region
DR Pattern Type: Customer-managed multi-region deployment and failover, validated per AI service
Same Region or Cross Region: Usually HA same region; DR cross region only with customer-managed design
Replication Type: Service-specific and often asynchronous or independently managed
Expected RTO Range: HA fit depends on the specific AI service and whether it supports replicas, zones, or managed failover in the chosen region
Expected RPO Range: RPO and RTO are rarely guaranteed at the umbrella level; they must be validated per service and per region
Recommended For: AI workloads that can standardize on explicit per-service DR runbooks and duplicate deployments
Not Recommended For: Teams looking for one generic Azure AI Services DR answer across all cognitive and AI products
Key Assumptions: The chosen AI service supports the target region pair and model or feature parity
Dependencies: Regional support, model availability, identity, networking, dependent data stores, app failover logic
Limitations: Documentation is service-specific and incomplete at the umbrella level, so recommendations here are inferred from official per-service guidance
Test / Drill Guidance: Test regional failover at the application layer and verify service parity, quotas, and output quality in the target region
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Baseline Azure AI Foundry chat reference architecture
Notes / Interpretation: This is intentionally labeled as inferred because Microsoft publishes AI reliability guidance primarily per service, not as one umbrella contract.

Deployment Model / SKU: Standard or Premium tier cluster with zonal node pools
HA Capability Summary: AKS control plane is zone resilient by default in zone-supporting regions, but cluster availability still depends on node pool design, pod replicas, topology spread, autoscaling, and resilient dependencies.
DR Capability Summary: AKS clusters are single-region resources. Regional DR requires separate clusters in another region, often coordinated with Fleet Manager or custom active-active or active-passive patterns, plus backup and restore for cluster resources and persistent volumes where needed.
HA Pattern Type: Zone-resilient cluster with multiple zonal node pools
DR Pattern Type: Multi-cluster multi-region deployment with Fleet Manager or custom orchestration
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: Cluster state plus external service replication; backups available for cluster resources and persistent volumes
Failover Type: Automatic in-region for configured zonal resilience; customer-managed for regional failover
Expected RTO Range: If zone-resilient best practices are followed, zone failure is not expected to cause workload downtime; region outage makes the cluster unavailable
Expected RPO Range: If using zone-redundant storage in-cluster, zone failure is not expected to cause data loss; regional DR RPO depends on backups and external data services
Recommended For: Container workloads that can invest in platform engineering and multi-cluster operations
Not Recommended For: Teams expecting control-plane resiliency alone to make the whole workload highly available
Key Assumptions: Pods are replicated, node pools span zones, autoscaling is configured, and dependencies are resilient
Dependencies: Ingress, storage, databases, backups, traffic routing, CI/CD for cluster recreation, Fleet Manager or equivalent
Limitations: AKS does not make a workload resilient by itself; single-cluster designs are not regional DR
Test / Drill Guidance: Test zone failure with node drain or Chaos Studio and run multi-region failover drills plus restore exercises
Notes / Interpretation: AKS HA is workload-architecture dependent even though the control plane is managed.

Deployment Model / SKU: Flex Consumption, Elastic Premium, Dedicated plans
Stateful or Stateless: Stateless and Stateful via Durable Functions
HA Capability Summary: Functions supports zone redundancy on Flex Consumption, Elastic Premium, and Dedicated/App Service plans, but the host storage account and Durable Functions state store are critical dependencies that must also meet resiliency needs. Consumption plan does not support availability zones.
DR Capability Summary: There is no built-in multi-region redundancy for a function app. Regional DR requires deploying the same code to multiple regions, using active-active with Front Door for critical HTTP triggers or active-passive for event-driven triggers with the paired messaging service handling failover.
HA Pattern Type: Zone-redundant function app on supported plan
DR Pattern Type: Customer-managed multi-region deployment; active-active for HTTP, active-passive for non-HTTP
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: Service-managed within plan plus dependency-specific replication
Failover Type: Automatic within supported zonal plan; customer-managed regional pattern
Expected RTO Range: Zone-redundant apps continue running during zone issues, but scale behavior can degrade on a best-effort basis in zonal incidents
Expected RPO Range: RPO depends on trigger source, host storage, and Durable Functions state provider; there is no uniform guarantee
Recommended For: Event-driven and HTTP workloads that can separate trigger patterns and duplicate deployments across regions
Not Recommended For: Teams assuming a single function app has built-in regional DR
Key Assumptions: Supported hosting plan is selected and all critical dependencies are regionally resilient
Dependencies: Storage account, trigger sources, Durable Functions state store, Front Door or messaging failover pattern
Limitations: Hosting plan matters greatly; active-passive is usually the safer pattern for non-HTTP triggers to avoid duplicate processing
Test / Drill Guidance: Drill HTTP failover through Front Door and event-driven failover through the paired messaging service and passive regional app
Notes / Interpretation: Functions HA and DR are highly trigger-pattern dependent.

HA Capability Summary: Key Vault provides built-in intra-region redundancy and automatically enables zone redundancy in regions that support availability zones, with no extra configuration required.
DR Capability Summary: Key Vault also replicates to a paired region in supported geographies and Microsoft may trigger failover on a best-effort basis, but failover can take hours and the secondary becomes read-only. For stronger DR objectives, use separate vaults in multiple regions with backup, restore, and application failover logic.
DR Pattern Type: Microsoft-managed paired-region failover or custom dual-vault design
Same Region or Cross Region: HA same region; DR cross region
Replication Type: Synchronous within region; asynchronous to paired region
Failover Type: Automatic in-region; Microsoft-managed or customer-managed cross-region
Expected RTO Range: Read operations should see minimal to no downtime in a zone failure; write operations can be temporarily affected during adjustments
Expected RPO Range: No data loss expected during zone failure; some data loss is possible in region failover if async replication lags
Recommended For: Applications that need highly available secret retrieval with moderate regional DR tolerance
Not Recommended For: Workloads needing tightly bounded regional RTO and RPO from Microsoft-managed failover alone
Key Assumptions: Region supports paired-region behavior or a custom second vault is deployed
Dependencies: Private Link, client retry handling, backup cadence, dual-vault routing logic
Limitations: Microsoft-managed regional failover is best-effort, can take several hours, and results in read-only secondary behavior
Test / Drill Guidance: Test secret retrieval during failover simulations, backup and restore procedures, and custom dual-vault failover logic
Notes / Interpretation: Key Vault has stronger built-in regional resilience than many services, but Microsoft-managed failover is not a low-RTO DR guarantee.

Deployment Model / SKU: Global Standard, Data Zone, Standard, PTU
HA Capability Summary: Azure OpenAI resources are regional. For higher resiliency, Microsoft recommends Global Standard deployments when possible, with Data Zone as the next best option for geographic-boundary processing requirements.
DR Capability Summary: If continuous availability matters, deploy at least two Azure OpenAI resources in different regions, duplicate model deployments, and place API Management or another AI gateway in front for load balancing, circuit breaking, and failover.
DR Pattern Type: Active-passive or split-workload multi-region design with gateway
Same Region or Cross Region: HA within a region is service-managed; DR cross region is customer-managed
Supports Availability Zones: Service-specific / not expressed as zone feature in source
Replication Type: Model deployments duplicated across resources and regions
Expected RTO Range: Regional endpoint availability is good for many workloads, but mission-critical continuity requires a secondary region and gateway layer
Expected RPO Range: RPO is generally near zero for stateless inference requests, but prompt history and agent state depend on external stores; throughput depends on quota and deployment type
Recommended For: AI applications that can deploy duplicate regional endpoints and manage gateway-based failover
Not Recommended For: Teams assuming one Azure OpenAI resource is a complete DR strategy
Key Assumptions: Matching model availability and quota exist in the failover region and gateway logic is implemented
Dependencies: Gateway or APIM, identity, quotas, content filters, dependent state stores such as Cosmos DB for agents
Limitations: Model and quota parity are critical; source guidance is portal-version-specific and strongly prefers Global Standard when available
Test / Drill Guidance: Test regional endpoint failover, gateway policies, quota exhaustion behavior, and dependency recovery for agent state
Microsoft Learn Diagram Title: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BCDR) considerations with Azure OpenAI in Azure AI Foundry Models
Notes / Interpretation: For Azure OpenAI, DR is an application and deployment-topology design, not a single-resource feature.
